channel.title: Terms on emsenn.net
channel.description: Recent content in Terms on emsenn.net

item.title: Emergent Obligation
item.description:
Emergent obligation is obligation that arises from situated relations rather than from pre-defined rules, and that shifts in real time as conditions change. It is not assigned by a framework, ratified by a procedure, or derived from a universal principle. It is generated by the relation itself — by what the relation requires in this moment, under these conditions, given what is at stake.

A grandmother falls on ice. The obligation to help her is not produced by a social contract, a deliberative assembly, or a moral calculus. It is produced by the relation: she is your grandmother, you are here, she is down. The obligation is immediate, specific, and non-transferable. It does not wait for justification. It does not require that helping her be universalizable. It does not ask whether everyone in comparable situations would be helped. The relation generates the obligation and the obligation generates the action.

item.title: Ethics of Presence
item.description:
An ethics of presence evaluates action by its adequacy to the present situation — the needs, relations, and constraints of this moment — rather than by its contribution toward a projected future or its conformity to an inherited ideal. You act for the relations that exist, not for the world you hope to build.

The concept draws on two converging sources. Vine Deloria Jr. contrasts Indigenous spatial-ethical thought with Western linear futurity: where Western political frameworks organize action around a timeline — progress, development, the arc of history — Indigenous thought grounds obligation in place and present relationship. The relevant question is not “does this advance our goals?” but “does this answer what the situation requires?” Glen Coulthard’s recognition refusal operates from the same temporal ground: Indigenous peoples need not orient their governance toward a settler-defined future in order for that governance to be real.

item.title: Tragic Realism
item.description:
Tragic realism is the acceptance that not everyone can be saved and that this is an ecological fact rather than a moral failure. It permits communities to make decisions about protection and priority under conditions where resources, time, and capacity are finite — without collapsing into the procedural paralysis that universalist frameworks generate when confronted with the impossibility of equal treatment.

The concept operates at the intersection of anarcho-nihilism and collapse adaptation. Anarcho-nihilism, as developed by Serafinski in Blessed Is the Flame (2016), demonstrates that resistance does not require hope — the intolerability of domination is sufficient grounds for action regardless of outcome. Tragic realism extends this into collective survival: adaptation does not require the guarantee that everyone will be adapted for. When shelter holds 350 and the neighborhood holds 1,000, the framework that insists on universal inclusion before action produces no action at all. Tragic realism permits the decision.

item.title: Desire
item.description: Desire is the mode of wanting organized around what you do not have. You desire what is absent — the revolution that has not yet come, the justice that does not yet exist, the freedom you have not yet achieved. Desire is structured by lack: it targets an object, pursues it, and is sustained by the gap between the wanting and the having. If the object were obtained, desire would not be satisfied — it would relocate to a new object, because desire is not really about the object at all. It is about the movement toward what is missing.

item.title: Drive
item.description: Drive is the mode of wanting that does not aim at an object. Where desire targets what is absent — the revolution, the better world, the thing you do not yet have — drive operates without a target. It repeats. It circles. Its movement is its own purpose. Lacan described the drive as a circuit: it goes out toward an object, does not reach it, returns, and goes out again. The satisfaction of drive is not in reaching the destination but in the going.

item.title: Hope
item.description: Hope, in the anarcho-nihilist analysis, is not a virtue but a requirement — and the requirement is the problem. The demand that activists, organizers, and resisters maintain hope — believe that a better world is possible, that things will improve, that the arc of history bends toward justice — functions as a precondition for political engagement. If you cannot produce hope, you are excluded from the struggle. Your honest assessment of conditions is reframed as defeatism. Your trauma is reframed as a personal obstacle to be overcome. Your analysis — that ecological collapse is accelerating, that state power is consolidating, that capitalism adapts faster than its opponents — is treated as something to be argued out of rather than engaged with.

item.title: Jouissance
item.description: Jouissance is the excessive pleasure that resistance produces independent of its outcomes. It is not happiness (which requires that things go well), not satisfaction (which requires that a need be met), not even joy in the ordinary sense (which implies something to be joyful about). It is the intensity of the act of refusal itself — the experience that exceeds any calculation of costs and benefits, that cannot be justified by what it achieves, and that persists even when what it achieves is nothing. This is the affective ground of anarcho-nihilism: you do not resist because you expect to win, and yet resistance is not joyless. Something happens in the act that exceeds the act’s instrumental content. That something is jouissance.

item.title: The Death Drive
item.description: The death drive is the force that operates beyond the pleasure principle. Freud introduced it in Beyond the Pleasure Principle (1920) to account for phenomena that his earlier theory could not explain: the compulsion to repeat traumatic experiences, the pursuit of situations that produce suffering rather than satisfaction, the tendency of organisms to seek not equilibrium but dissolution. He proposed a fundamental duality: Eros (the life drives) binds, connects, and builds complexity; Thanatos (the death drive) unbinds, disintegrates, and tends toward the dissolution of every organized structure.

item.title: Anarcho-Nihilism
item.description:
Anarcho-nihilism is the political orientation that acts against domination without requiring hope, progress, or the expectation of success as preconditions for action. It combines anarchism’s opposition to all forms of domination with nihilism’s refusal to ground action in transcendent meaning, historical progress, or faith in a better future.

The position was developed most explicitly by Serafinski in Blessed is the Flame (2016), which examines resistance within Nazi concentration camps — sabotage, revolt, escape, refusal — as political acts that cannot be evaluated by their prospects for success. The concentration camp is the limit case: resistance persists where victory is impossible, where no program can be implemented, where the future offers nothing. Serafinski argues that this is not an aberration but a revelation of what resistance has always been — an act grounded in present conditions, not future expectations.
